eras.name: Gaucho & Rural Traditions
eras.description: The gaucho horsemen of the Pampas developed a distinctive dress code built for life on the open plains. Their clothing combined Spanish colonial influences with practical innovations, creating enduring symbols of Argentine national identity.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A weathered Argentine gaucho on horseback wearing traditional bombachas tucked into leather boots, a striped wool poncho draped over one shoulder, and a wide-brimmed hat, vast Pampas grassland at golden hour.

eras.name: Belle Époque Porteña
eras.description: Buenos Aires became the 'Paris of South America' as wealthy Argentine elites imported Parisian couture and European tailoring traditions. The tango milieu simultaneously created its own sartorial codes of sharp suits and dramatic dresses.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 1920s Buenos Aires tango salon with a couple dancing, the woman in a beaded silk dress with a dramatic thigh-high slit, the man in a sharp pinstripe suit and fedora, moody amber lighting and marble columns.

eras.name: Evita & Post-War Glamour
eras.description: Eva Perón's transformation from modest actress to impeccably dressed First Lady made her a global fashion icon. Her wardrobe of Dior gowns and tailored suits symbolized both aspiration and political power for the Argentine working class.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A glamorous 1950s portrait of a woman resembling Eva Perón in a structured ivory Dior suit with gold brooch, her blonde hair swept into a chignon, waving from a balcony overlooking a Buenos Aires plaza.

eras.name: Di Tella Avant-Garde
eras.description: The Instituto Di Tella fostered an explosive countercultural movement in Buenos Aires. Argentine designers and artists embraced pop art, op art, and radical experimentation, creating a brief but intense period of fashion innovation.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 1960s Buenos Aires pop-art scene featuring a young woman in a bold black and white op-art print minidress with white vinyl boots, standing in a colorful gallery installation, film grain texture.

eras.name: Post-Crisis Creative Boom
eras.description: The 2001 economic crisis paradoxically unleashed a wave of design creativity. Unable to afford imports, a generation of designers built independent labels using local materials, upcycling, and distinctly Argentine aesthetics.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Palermo Soho boutique scene with industrial exposed brick, displaying a collection of deconstructed leather jackets and hand-printed cotton garments in muted earth tones, natural afternoon light.

eras.name: New Buenos Aires Wave
eras.description: Buenos Aires has cemented its position as South America's fashion capital with designers like Jazmin Chebar, Trosman, and Kostüme gaining international recognition. The city blends European sophistication with Latin American warmth and creative risk-taking.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Buenos Aires Fashion Week runway with a model wearing oversized pleated gaucho-inspired trousers in cream linen paired with a structured black blazer, industrial warehouse setting with dramatic side lighting.

textiles.name: Poncho Pampeano
textiles.description: A heavy wool poncho with distinctive stripe patterns that vary by province, serving as both garment and blanket on the Pampas.
textiles.technique: Criollo loom weaving using hand-spun sheep wool with natural plant dyes

textiles.name: Argentine Leather
textiles.description: World-renowned for its suppleness and quality, Argentine cowhide leather supports a major fashion and accessories industry.
textiles.technique: Vegetable tanning using quebracho bark extract, hand-finishing and embossing

textiles.name: Barracán
textiles.description: A thick, waterproof wool cloth used for gaucho ponchos and riding coats, tightly woven to resist Pampas rain and wind.
textiles.technique: Tight plain weave on pedal loom with lanolin-rich unscoured wool for natural water resistance

textiles.name: Andean Aguayo
textiles.description: A vibrantly colored cloth from the northwestern Andean region, used as a carrying cloth, blanket, and decorative textile with pre-Columbian geometric motifs.
textiles.technique: Backstrap and pedal loom weaving with complementary warp patterning in llama and sheep wool

climate.fashionImplication: Buenos Aires' mild but changeable climate with cool winters drives demand for leather jackets, layered knitwear, and versatile trans-seasonal pieces, while Patagonian cold inspires rugged outerwear.

culturalInfluences: Gaucho horseman tradition and rural estancia life
Tango culture and its codes of elegance
Italian and Spanish immigrant tailoring traditions
Eva Perón as political fashion icon
Football culture and casual sportswear influence
Economic crisis creativity and anti-establishment design

emergingTrends: Sustainable leather alternatives from cactus and mushroom
Digital-first fashion brands bypassing traditional retail
